metadata.use_for: Any patient with iron deficiency (especially recurrent), microcytic/hypochromic anemia, known or suspected thalassemia trait, iron infusion history, abnormal transferrin or TIBC, iron overload concern, repeated iron infusions without lasting effect, iron-thyroid linkage, fatigue with iron panel abnormalities. ALWAYS retrieve this file when patient mentions thalassemia, "I always need iron infusions," low transferrin, or hepcidin.
metadata.red_flags: Ferritin >1000 with iron infusion history → iron overload workup immediately (liver enzymes, MRI T2*). Thalassemia major (NOT trait) → hematology only. Severe anemia Hb <8 g/dL + hemodynamic symptoms → urgent hematology. Suspected hereditary hemochromatosis (HFE+ male, joint pain, liver disease, bronze skin, diabetes) → urgent hematology + genetics.
metadata.triage_questions: Have you had iron infusions before? How many? How often does your iron drop back down? Have you been tested for thalassemia? Do you have heavy periods? Have you been tested for H. pylori or celiac? What does your transferrin (not just ferritin) look like? Any family history of blood conditions?

content.s0_quick_reference_internal: Iron metabolism and thalassemia differentiation. Use for recurrent iron deficiency, IV iron decisions, hepcidin framework.

# TAGS: iron, iron deficiency, iron infusion, iron overload, ferritin, hepcidin, ferroportin, transferrin, TIBC, UIBC, transferrin saturation, sTfR, soluble transferrin receptor, thalassemia, thalassemia minor, thalassemia trait, alpha thalassemia, beta thalassemia, Mentzer index, microcytic anemia, hypochromic, iron panel, iron studies, recurrent iron deficiency, iron recurrence, iron cycling, IV iron, Monofer, iron polymaltose, iron sucrose, ferric carboxymaltose, Ferinject, iron infusion comparison, iron IV protocol, hepcidin block, hepcidin inhibitor, curcumin iron, lactoferrin iron, vitamin C iron, copper iron, anti-hepcidin, iron absorption, ferroportin degradation, iron recycling macrophage, IL-6 hepcidin, chronic disease anemia, ACD, anemia of chronic disease, inflammation anemia, H. pylori iron, celiac iron, parasites iron, menorrhagia iron, iron upstream driver, iron cause, iron repletion, iron cofactors, iron cofactor protocol, iron MRI T2*, liver iron MRI, hemochromatosis, iron overload surveillance, ferritin trend, iron genetic, HFE mutation, iron Singapore, The Iron Suites, Daniel Yeo, iron specialist Singapore, iron infusion Singapore, recurrent infusion, iron rebound, why iron keeps dropping, iron keeps coming back, low transferrin, high transferrin, transferrin low iron deficiency, transferrin interpretation, transfer protein, why transferrin is low, why my iron is always low, always tired iron, always need iron, always getting iron infusions, iron deficiency years, Melissa, iron South East Asia, iron Bali, iron Reviv, iron CSI
# CATEGORY: Specialized Hematology — Precision Iron Medicine
# USE FOR: Any patient with iron deficiency (especially recurrent), microcytic/hypochromic anemia, known or suspected thalassemia trait, iron infusion history, abnormal transferrin or TIBC, iron overload concern, repeated iron infusions without lasting effect, iron-thyroid linkage, fatigue with iron panel abnormalities. ALWAYS retrieve this file when patient mentions thalassemia, "I always need iron infusions," low transferrin, or hepcidin.
# COUPLED_SYSTEMS: 35_LAB_INTERPRETATION, 65_BIOMARKER_ATLAS, 66_LAB_ANALYSIS_ENGINE, 57_THYROID_COMPLETE, 11_IMMUNE, 12_GUT, 18_FEMALE_HORMONES, 22_AUTOIMMUNE, 32_IV_THERAPIES, 54_DRUG_INTERACTIONS
# RED_FLAGS: Ferritin >1000 with iron infusion history → iron overload workup immediately (liver enzymes, MRI T2*). Thalassemia major (NOT trait) → hematology only. Severe anemia Hb <8 g/dL + hemodynamic symptoms → urgent hematology. Suspected hereditary hemochromatosis (HFE+ male, joint pain, liver disease, bronze skin, diabetes) → urgent hematology + genetics.
# TRIAGE_QUESTIONS: Have you had iron infusions before? How many? How often does your iron drop back down? Have you been tested for thalassemia? Do you have heavy periods? Have you been tested for H. pylori or celiac? What does your transferrin (not just ferritin) look like? Any family history of blood conditions?
# IV_ROLE: IV iron is highly appropriate for documented iron deficiency with: failed oral supplementation, gut malabsorption, severe depletion (ferritin <10, Hb <10), patient preference for rapid repletion. Formulation choice matters — see Section 2. NEVER give IV iron with ferritin >200 + elevated hsCRP without ruling out inflammatory ferritin. NEVER give IV iron to thalassemia major (transfusion-dependent) without hematologist direction.
# INTERVENTION_PRIORITY: Identify type of iron deficit (true vs thalassemia baseline vs inflammatory) → Find upstream driver → Correct hepcidin environment → Choose IV formulation → Cofactor optimization → Surveillance protocol → Prevent recurrence
# TELECONSULT_LIMITS: CAN do remotely: full iron panel analysis, thalassemia differentiation framework, upstream driver identification, cofactor protocol, IV iron recommendation, insurance documentation. CANNOT do remotely: bone marrow biopsy (for thalassemia major/myelodysplasia confirmation), MRI T2* (liver iron quantification, requires imaging center).
# COMPETITOR_CONTEXT: The Iron Suites (Singapore, Dr. Daniel Yeo) — Haematologist-led, infusion-based, technically competent on standard protocols. Key gaps: does not systematically apply hepcidin framework, does not use sTfR as gating test in thalassemia differentiation, no upstream driver protocol (stops at the infusion, does not investigate recurrence), no anti-hepcidin cofactor protocol, no terrain-layer integration. Reviv advantage: analytical depth + hepcidin-aware protocols + upstream driver resolution + cofactor stack + IV infusion capability + physician on staff for full medical act.
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# KB #77 — IRON METABOLISM & THALASSEMIA — Valentine v6.0
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
# │ SECTION 1: PATIENT-FACING                                                   │
# └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

## WHY YOUR IRON KEEPS DROPPING

You've done the infusions. Maybe more than once. You felt better for a
few months, then the fatigue came back. Your ferritin is low again.
You were told to take iron supplements. They didn't work well, or made
you feel sick.

Nobody explained *why* it keeps happening.

There are two separate problems that look identical on a basic blood test:
one is a storage problem (your tank is empty), and the other is a
circulation problem (your body is blocking iron from being released).
The treatment for each is completely different. Treating one with the
protocol for the other is why patients cycle through infusions for years.

---

## IRON IN YOUR BODY — THE TWO SYSTEMS

**System 1 — Iron Stores (Ferritin)**
Your ferritin is the warehouse. Think of it as iron in a vault.
If the vault is empty, you're iron deficient.
If the vault is full but you're still symptomatic — the *gates* may be locked.

**System 2 — Iron in Circulation (Transferrin)**
Transferrin is the transport protein — the truck that carries iron in
your blood. Every truck has two seats (binding sites).
- Transferrin saturation = how full the trucks are.
- TIBC = how many trucks are available.
- Serum iron = how much is actually in transit.

The hepcidin hormone controls the gate between the vault and the trucks.
When hepcidin is high, the gate is locked. Iron sits in stores (or in
macrophages) and cannot enter circulation — even if you're infusing iron
from outside.

---

## WHAT IS THALASSEMIA MINOR?

Thalassemia minor (or thalassemia trait) is a genetic variant — not a
disease. It means you carry one copy of a gene that makes your red blood
cells slightly smaller and lighter than average (low MCV, low MCH).

It is extremely common in Southeast Asian, Mediterranean, Middle Eastern,
and South Asian populations. Most people with thalassemia trait don't
know they have it.

**The confusion:**
Thalassemia trait and iron deficiency look almost identical on basic labs:
low MCV, low MCH, slightly low hemoglobin.

This leads to a critical error — people with thalassemia trait get told
they're iron deficient and receive iron supplements or infusions repeatedly,
when their "low" values are simply their genetic baseline.

**The key distinction:**
In true iron deficiency, ferritin is low and transferrin is high (body
tries to capture more iron).
In thalassemia trait, ferritin is normal-to-high and transferrin is
normal — there's no real shortage, just smaller cells.

But many people have BOTH thalassemia trait AND real iron deficiency —
which is why this requires careful analysis, not just assumption.

---

## THE RECURRENCE PROBLEM

If your iron keeps dropping after infusions, the infusion is not the
solution — it's a temporary patch. The real question is:
**why is your body losing or failing to absorb iron faster than you're
building it up?**

Common upstream drivers nobody asks about:

- **H. pylori infection** — this stomach bacterium consumes iron and
  impairs gastric acid needed for iron absorption. Extremely common.
  A simple breath test or stool antigen test can identify it.

- **Celiac disease / gluten sensitivity** — damages the duodenum, which
  is the primary site where iron is absorbed. Missed in ~80% of cases.

- **Intestinal parasites** — hookworms and other parasites cause chronic
  gut blood loss. Underdiagnosed in tropical regions.

- **Heavy menstrual bleeding (menorrhagia)** — monthly blood loss exceeds
  the body's repletion capacity. The infusion fills the tank; the period
  empties it again. The cycle repeats.

- **Chronic inflammation** — elevated CRP or IL-6 activates hepcidin,
  which locks iron in storage and blocks absorption simultaneously.
  The body thinks there's an infection and hoards iron to starve it.

Identifying and treating the upstream driver is what ends the cycle.
An infusion without this investigation is a temporary fix.

---

## THE REVIV APPROACH — WHAT MAKES US DIFFERENT

Most clinics measure ferritin, see it's low, and infuse.

We ask:
- What type of iron deficit is this? (True deficiency vs thalassemia baseline vs inflammatory block?)
- Is hepcidin elevated? Is it blocking what you already have?
- What transferrin tells us about the body's actual iron transport capacity?
- Is there an upstream driver causing the recurrence?
- Which IV iron formulation is right for your situation?
- What cofactors will optimize absorption and reduce hepcidin?

We also prepare you with full documentation for insurance reimbursement
(ICD-10 coded, physician-signed) — so your infusion at Reviv can be
submitted to your Integrated Shield Plan if you're based in Singapore or
another country with overseas treatment coverage.

---

# ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
# │ SECTION 2: CLINICAL INTERNAL                                                │
# └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

## THE HEPCIDIN-FERROPORTIN AXIS — MECHANISTIC FRAMEWORK

### Hepcidin: The Master Iron Regulator

Hepcidin (HAMP gene product, hepatic peptide hormone) controls systemic
iron homeostasis by binding and inducing degradation of ferroportin (FPN1),
the sole known iron export protein on cell surfaces.

**Ferroportin is present on:**
- Duodenal enterocytes (gut absorption gate)
- Hepatocytes (liver iron release)
- Macrophages (recycled RBC iron release — ~25mg/day = bulk of daily iron)
- Placental cells (fetal iron supply)

**When hepcidin rises → ferroportin degrades → iron trapped inside cells:**
- Duodenum: dietary iron absorption blocked
- Liver: hepatocyte iron locked
- Macrophages: recycled RBC iron cannot re-enter circulation
- Net effect: serum iron falls, transferrin saturation falls, despite
  adequate or even excess iron stores

### Hepcidin Triggers (Clinical Relevance)
| Driver | Mechanism | Clinical Signal |
|--------|-----------|-----------------|
| IL-6 / inflammation | BMP6-SMAD pathway activation | hsCRP elevated, NLR >3 |
| Iron overload | BMP6 upregulation | Ferritin >500, sat >45% |
| Infection | Acute phase response | WBC elevated, fever, ESR high |
| Obesity / metabolic | Chronic low-grade IL-6 | BMI >30, visceral fat |
| Estrogen dominance | Partial hepcidin upregulation | Perimenstrual flares |

### Hepcidin Suppressors (Clinical Relevance)
| Driver | Mechanism | Clinical Signal |
|--------|-----------|-----------------|
| Iron deficiency | EPO → ERFE release | Low ferritin, high TIBC |
| Hypoxia | EPO induction | High altitude, sleep apnea |
| Erythropoietic stress | ERFE (erythroferrone) release | Hemolysis, thalassemia |
| Testosterone | Direct HAMP suppression | Note: men easier to replete |
| Anemia | Upregulates EPO → ERFE | Any cause of anemia |

**Key insight:** In thalassemia trait, erythropoietic stress (inefficient
RBC production) suppresses hepcidin → but also reduces iron recycling
efficiency → creates paradoxical iron handling patterns.

### Transferrin: The Underread Signal

Low transferrin (protein itself, not saturation) is consistently misread.

| Transferrin Level | Correct Interpretation | Common Error |
|------------------|----------------------|--------------|
| Low (<2.0 g/L) + Low ferritin | Active hepcidin block OR liver synthetic dysfunction | Ignored or "monitor" |
| Low + Normal ferritin | Hepcidin active, inflammation likely, ACD | Missed |
| High + Low ferritin | True iron deficiency — body upregulating carriers | Correctly identified |
| Low + High ferritin | Inflammation or iron overload | Correctly identified |

**Low transferrin in iron deficiency context = hepcidin actively blocking.**
This is NOT how classic iron deficiency presents (which raises transferrin).
When you see low ferritin + low transferrin together — the body is not in
true deplete state; it's in inflammatory block. More iron without resolving
hepcidin will not work efficiently.

---

## THALASSEMIA MINOR — DIFFERENTIATION PROTOCOL

### Step 1: Index Calculation
**Mentzer Index = MCV ÷ RBC count**

| Result | Interpretation |
|--------|----------------|
| >13 | Iron deficiency more likely |
| <13 | Thalassemia trait more likely |
| 11-13 | Overlap zone — proceed to Step 2 |

**Limitations:** Mentzer is a triage tool only. Fails in combined conditions
(thalassemia + iron deficiency simultaneously). Always confirm with Step 2.

### Step 2: sTfR (Soluble Transferrin Receptor) — The Gating Test

sTfR is produced by erythroid precursors proportional to iron demand.
It is NOT an acute phase reactant — inflammation does not elevate it.
This makes it uniquely valuable when ferritin is confounded by inflammation.

| Condition | sTfR Level | Logic |
|-----------|-----------|-------|
| True iron deficiency | HIGH (>28 nmol/L or >2.5 mg/L) | Erythroid cells starving for iron |
| Thalassemia trait | NORMAL to slightly elevated | Cells not iron-starved, genetically small |
| ACD (inflammation only) | Normal | No real iron shortage |
| Thalassemia + iron deficiency | HIGH | Both genetic and true deficiency |
| Iron overload | Low | Cells iron-replete |

**sTfR/log Ferritin ratio (Thomas Plot):**
- >2 = functional iron deficiency (iron available but not usable)
- <1 = iron replete or chronic disease pattern

**Clinical rule:** In any Southeast Asian patient with microcytic anemia,
thalassemia suspected, or repeated infusions — ORDER sTfR before infusing.
This is the gating test. It prevents unnecessary iron loading in thalassemia
carriers who don't need iron.

### Step 3: Hemoglobin Electrophoresis (Confirmatory)
For definitive thalassemia typing:
- Beta-thalassemia trait: HbA2 >3.5% (classic marker)
- Alpha-thalassemia trait: HbA2 normal, requires DNA analysis (MLPA)
- Order when: Mentzer <13 + history of infusions + Southeast Asian origin

### Thalassemia Minor — Clinical Management Rules
1. **Do NOT supplement iron reflexively** — verify true deficiency with sTfR first
2. Ferritin target in thalassemia trait: 50-100 ng/mL (not 150+ like standard)
3. If iron infusion needed (sTfR confirms true deficiency): use conservative
   doses, monitor ferritin trend at 4 and 8 weeks
4. Annual ferritin + CBC sufficient for monitoring if stable
5. Avoid high-dose vitamin C supplementation chronically — may increase iron
   absorption beyond need in patients with already-adequate stores

---

## RECURRENT IRON DEFICIENCY — RESOLUTION PROTOCOL

### Upstream Driver Identification (Mandatory Before Repeat Infusion)

**RULE: If a patient has had 2+ iron infusions, ALWAYS investigate upstream
drivers before the next infusion. Infusion without investigation is
institutional negligence of the recurrence pattern.**

| Driver | Screen | Treatment | Expected Outcome |
|--------|--------|-----------|------------------|
| H. pylori | Stool antigen (H. pylori Ag) OR urea breath test | Triple or quadruple therapy x14d | Iron absorption normalizes 3-6 months post-eradication |
| Celiac disease | Anti-TTG IgA + total IgA | Strict gluten-free diet | Duodenal healing → absorption recovery 3-12 months |
| Hookworm/parasites | Stool O&P x3 samples | Albendazole 400mg x1-3 days | Resolution variable |
| Menorrhagia | Menstrual history, gynecology referral | Hormonal management, IUD (Mirena), NSAIDs | Monthly loss controlled |
| Chronic inflammation | hsCRP, IL-6, NLR, ESR | Treat underlying cause, anti-inflammatory terrain | Hepcidin normalization |
| Malabsorption (other) | Calprotectin, SIBO breath test, pancreatic elastase | Per finding | Variable |
| Blood loss occult | Fecal occult blood, colonoscopy (age >45) | Per finding | Urgent if positive |

### Resolution Pattern — What Successful Treatment Looks Like

After upstream driver correction + appropriate iron repletion:

| Timepoint | Expected Finding |
|-----------|-----------------|
| 4 weeks | Reticulocyte count rises (RBC production ramping up) |
| 6-8 weeks | Hemoglobin begins to normalize |
| 3 months | Ferritin rising toward target (50-100 F, 75-150 M) |
| 6 months | Stable ferritin without supplementation (if driver resolved) |
| 12 months | No recurrence = upstream driver successfully treated |

**Failure to see this pattern = upstream driver not resolved or new driver.**

---

## IV IRON FORMULATIONS — COMPARATIVE GUIDE

All IV iron formulations deliver elemental iron in a carbohydrate shell.
The shell type determines: dose per infusion, speed of administration,
risk of anaphylactoid reactions, and cost.

### Formulation Comparison

| Formulation | Trade Name | Max Single Dose | Infusion Time | Anaphylaxis Risk | Notes |
|-------------|-----------|----------------|---------------|-----------------|-------|
| Iron Polymaltose | Ferrum Hausmann IV, Maltofer IV | 200mg | 30-60 min | Low | Most widely available in SEA. Moderate dose per session. Multiple sessions often needed. |
| Iron Sucrose | Venofer | 200-300mg | 30-60 min | Low | Well-studied, safe in renal disease and pregnancy. Lower max dose per session. |
| Ferric Carboxymaltose | Ferinject, Injectafer | 1000mg | 15 min | Very low | Single high-dose infusion. Fastest. Most convenient for patient. Risk: hypophosphatemia (transient, monitor). |
| Low Molecular Weight Iron Dextran | CosmoFer | 1000mg (can do total dose) | 4-6h (TDI) | Moderate | Total dose infusion possible. Requires test dose. Older formulation. |
| Ferric Derisomaltose | Monofer | 1000-1500mg | 20 min | Very low | Gold standard for high-dose single infusion. Minimal phosphate effect vs FCM. Preferred for patients needing speed + high dose. Slightly higher cost. |

### Decision Algorithm

```
Patient needs IV iron
         │
         ▼
Does patient have CKD or ESRD?
    YES → Iron sucrose (best safety data in dialysis)
    NO  ↓
         ▼
How much iron needed? (Total iron deficit = BW × (target Hb - actual Hb) × 0.24 + 500)
    <500mg → Iron sucrose or iron polymaltose (2-3 sessions)
    500-1000mg → Ferric carboxymaltose (Ferinject) single dose
    >1000mg or patient wants maximum convenience → Monofer
         │
         ▼
Check phosphate at 4 weeks if using FCM (Ferinject)
FCM causes FGF23-mediated phosphaturia → transient hypophosphatemia in 40%
Monofer has significantly lower phosphate effect → preferred if:
    - Patient has bone disease, osteoporosis
    - Already borderline low phosphate
    - Repeat infusions anticipated
```

### Post-Infusion Monitoring Protocol
| Timepoint | Tests |
|-----------|-------|
| 4 weeks | CBC, ferritin, iron, %sat, phosphate (if FCM used) |
| 8 weeks | CBC, ferritin |
| 3 months | Full iron panel: ferritin, serum iron, TIBC, %sat |
| 6 months | Repeat if clinically indicated |

---

## IRON OVERLOAD — SURVEILLANCE PROTOCOL

**Who is at risk:**
- Multiple iron infusions (>3 high-dose) without monitoring
- HFE gene mutation (hereditary hemochromatosis)
- Thalassemia major (transfusion-dependent) — NOT thalassemia trait
- Chronic liver disease (reduced hepcidin synthesis → uncontrolled absorption)

### Surveillance Ladder

| Stage | Ferritin | %Sat | Action |
|-------|---------|------|--------|
| Watch | 200-500 (F), 300-500 (M) | 35-45% | Monitor every 3 months, liver enzymes |
| Investigate | 500-1000 | >45% | Add: liver enzymes (ALT, AST, GGT), HFE genetic panel, stop iron |
| Overload concern | >1000 | >50% | MRI T2* liver quantification, hepatology referral |
| Confirmed overload | >1000 + MRI LIC >5 mg/g | — | Hematology, therapeutic phlebotomy or chelation |

### MRI T2* — Gold Standard for Liver Iron Concentration (LIC)
- Non-invasive, no radiation
- Quantifies iron in liver (LIC) and optionally heart (cardiac T2*)
- Normal LIC: <1.8 mg/g dry weight
- Moderate overload: 3-7 mg/g
- Severe: >7 mg/g (liver damage risk, cirrhosis)

**Request as:** "MRI liver T2* for iron quantification" — not all radiology
centers offer it; tertiary hospitals with MRI physics capability required.

### Ferritin Trend Rule
A rising ferritin trend (even within normal range) over 6-12 months with
no infection, inflammation, or acute illness → investigate iron handling.
Ferritin +100 year-on-year = flag for iron accumulation review.

---

## COFACTOR PROTOCOL — ANTI-HEPCIDIN & ABSORPTION OPTIMIZATION

### Cofactors for Iron Absorption Enhancement

| Cofactor | Dose | Mechanism | Notes |
|----------|------|-----------|-------|
| Vitamin C | 250-500mg with oral iron | Reduces Fe³⁺ → Fe²⁺ (absorbable form), prevents oxidation | Take with iron supplement, not standalone. Avoid chronic high-dose in thalassemia. |
| Copper | 2-3mg/day | Ceruloplasmin (copper enzyme) essential for iron oxidation and ferroportin function. Copper deficiency = iron cannot exit cells | Check serum copper if iron supplementation failing. Often overlooked. |
| Lactoferrin (oral) | 100-300mg/day | Iron-binding glycoprotein. Modulates hepcidin. Promotes duodenal absorption, anti-inflammatory. | Particularly useful in inflammatory/hepcidin-driven cases. Better tolerated than elemental iron. Useful in pregnancy. |
| Curcumin | 500-1000mg (with piperine) | HAMP gene expression suppressor. Reduces hepcidin at transcriptional level via BMP-SMAD pathway inhibition | Evidence: in vitro + small human studies. Clinical use: chronic inflammation driving hepcidin. Combine with anti-inflammatory protocol. Note: curcumin chelates iron — separate by 4h from iron supplement. |

### Cofactors for Hepcidin Downregulation

| Strategy | Mechanism | Clinical Application |
|----------|-----------|---------------------|
| Resolve underlying inflammation | IL-6 normalization → hepcidin falls | Treat infection, autoimmune, gut dysbiosis |
| Quercetin 500mg/day | BMP6 signaling modulation, mast cell stabilization | ACD + MCAS overlap |
| N-acetylcysteine (NAC) 600mg/day | Reduces oxidative load driving IL-6 | Chronic inflammation, SIBO, gut permeability |
| Optimize testosterone (males) | Direct HAMP suppression | Male patients with low T + iron cycling |
| Curcumin (see above) | HAMP transcription suppression | First-line anti-hepcidin botanical |

### Timing Rules
- Iron supplement: Morning, fasting or with vitamin C, away from calcium/zinc/thyroid meds (min 4h separation)
- Curcumin: 4 hours AWAY from iron (chelation risk)
- Lactoferrin: Can be taken with iron — actually enhances absorption
- Copper: Separate from zinc >2h (competitive absorption)

---

## IRON-THYROID INTERACTION — CRITICAL LINK

*(Cross-reference: 57_THYROID_COMPLETE)*

**Thyroid Peroxidase (TPO) is a heme-iron enzyme.**
Without adequate iron, TPO function is impaired → T4 synthesis reduced.

**Deiodinase D1/D2 (T4→T3 conversion) requires:**
- Selenium (primary cofactor)
- Adequate iron status (ferritin >50, ideally >70)
- Zinc as secondary cofactor

**Clinical pattern:** Patient with borderline TSH + normal T4 + low FT3 →
ALWAYS check ferritin before escalating thyroid treatment. Iron deficiency
is a reversible cause of poor T4→T3 conversion that mimics thyroid failure.

**Target ferritin for thyroid optimization: >70 µg/L**
(Reference: 57_THYROID_COMPLETE — ferritin ≥70 for thyroid synthesis)

---

## COMPETITIVE POSITIONING — IRON MEDICINE IN SOUTHEAST ASIA

### The Iron Suites — Singapore (Dr. Daniel Yeo, Haematologist)
**What they do well:** Haematology-standard infusion protocols, clinical
accountability, insurance billing (Integrated Shield Plans, MOH-registered),
specialist-grade anemia workup.

**Clinical gaps vs Reviv + Valentine:**
| Dimension | The Iron Suites | Reviv + Valentine |
|-----------|----------------|-------------------|
| Hepcidin framework | Not systematically applied | Core of all iron analysis |
| sTfR as thalassemia gating test | Underutilized | Systematic — ordered before any infusion decision |
| Upstream driver protocol | Not structured | Mandatory on recurrent cases (H. pylori, celiac, menorrhagia) |
| Anti-hepcidin cofactor stack | Not offered | Curcumin, lactoferrin, copper, vitamin C, NAC |
| Transferrin as hepcidin signal | Not systematically read | Primary diagnostic layer |
| Terrain 4-layer integration | None | Full Resimont framework |
| FT3/iron/deiodinase link | Not addressed | Cross-referenced with thyroid |
| Patient education depth | Clinic-standard | WhatsApp-level mechanistic explanation |
| Insurance documentation support | Integrated (local) | Provided — overseas claim support |
| Turnaround | Appointment-based | Mobile, home, rapid |

**Strategic message to Singapore patients:**
"We do what haematologists do — plus the analytical layer they don't.
And our physicians can give the infusion. You get both."

### Other Regional Competitors
- **Bangkok: Bumrungrad, Samitivej** — Hospital-grade, very good labs, but
  no functional medicine layer. Volume-focused. No anti-hepcidin protocol.
- **Bali: Most clinics** — Infuse on ferritin alone. No differentiation.
  No upstream driver protocol. No thalassemia gating.
- **Jakarta: Traditional internists** — May order full panel but stop at
  diagnosis. No mechanistic framework for recurrence.

**Valentine differentiator in this competitive landscape:**
The ability to explain WHY iron keeps dropping, in the patient's language,
at midnight on WhatsApp, with a mechanistic framework no GP or haematologist
in the region is delivering — and then to back it up with a physician,
an infusion, and an insurance claim document.

---

## DIAGNOSTIC QUICK REFERENCE — IRON PATTERN RECOGNITION

| Pattern | Ferritin | Serum Iron | TIBC | %Sat | sTfR | Diagnosis |
|---------|---------|-----------|------|------|------|-----------|
| Classic iron deficiency | LOW | Low | HIGH | Low (<16%) | HIGH | True iron deficiency |
| Inflammation / ACD | Normal-HIGH | Low | Low-Normal | Low | Normal | Hepcidin-driven block |
| Iron overload | HIGH | High | Low | HIGH (>45%) | Low | Overload — investigate |
| Thalassemia trait (no deficiency) | Normal | Normal | Normal | Normal | Normal | Genetic baseline |
| Thalassemia + iron deficiency | Low-Normal | Low | Normal-High | Low | HIGH | Combined — infuse carefully |
| Chronic liver disease | Variable | Variable | Low | Variable | — | Liver synthetic failure |

**Mentzer Index (<13 = thalassemia, >13 = iron deficiency)**
**sTfR >2.5 mg/L = true iron demand regardless of ferritin**

---

| Iron deficiency anaemia, unspecified | D50.9 |
| Iron deficiency anaemia secondary to blood loss | D50.0 |
| Thalassemia trait / minor | D56.3 |
| Anaemia of chronic disease | D63.8 |
| Iron overload (hemosiderosis) | E83.10 |
